title: podman uses wrong pid(?)
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

I don't think this is a bug, but maybe it should be? Or am I doing something wrong here? Using `su` to switch user, and podman uses the original user's pid resulting in errors (rootless).

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create RHEL 8 vm or machine

2. A normal user is created (pid 1000)

3. ssh into the machine with this normal user

4. Create another user `sudo useradd ...`

5. Set the password `sudo passwd ...`

6. Switch to the user `su another`

7. Execute any podman command

**Describe the results you received:**

Errors, as podman is trying to create directories with wrong pid:

```none
ERRO[0000] cannot mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: permission denied 
ERRO[0000] cannot mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: permission denied 
ERRO[0000] failed to setup From and Bud flags: failed to get container config: cannot mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: permission denied 
ERRO[0000] cannot mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: permission denied 
ERRO[0000] cannot mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: permission denied 
ERRO[0000] cannot mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: permission denied 
ERRO[0000] cannot mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: permission denied 
ERRO[0000] cannot mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: permission denied 
ERRO[0000] cannot mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: permission denied 
ERRO[0000] cannot mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: mkdir /run/user/1000/libpod: permission denied 
```
**Describe the results you expected:**

I expected it to work normally?

```
[retool@localhost onpremise]$ id
uid=1001(retool) gid=1001(retool) groups=1001(retool) context=unconfined_u:unconfined_r:unconfined_t:s0-s0:c0.c1023
[retool@localhost onpremise]$ whoami
retool
```

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

If I exit the session, and then ssh as that user; and then type `/bin/bash`, it works.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
[burhan@localhost ~]$ podman info --debug
debug:
  compiler: gc
  gitCommit: ""
  goVersion: go1.13.4   
  podmanVersion: 1.9.3  
host:
  arch: amd64
  buildahVersion: 1.14.9
  cgroupVersion: v1     
  conmon:
    package: conmon-2.0.17-1.module+el8.2.1+6771+3533eb4c.x86_64
    path: /usr/bin/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 2.0.17, commit: 3c703d9f178a3a53966e1d5c03d0275ea6cb36a0'
  cpus: 1
  distribution:
    distribution: '"rhel"'
    version: "8.2"
  eventLogger: file
  hostname: localhost.localdomain
  idMappings:
    gidmap:
    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 1000
      size: 1
    - container_id: 1
      host_id: 100000
      size: 65536
    uidmap:
    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 1000
      size: 1
    - container_id: 1
      host_id: 100000
      size: 65536
  kernel: 4.18.0-193.el8.x86_64
  memFree: 5599051776
  memTotal: 8189816832
  ociRuntime:
    name: runc
    package: runc-1.0.0-66.rc10.module+el8.2.1+6465+1a51e8b6.x86_64
    path: /usr/bin/runc
    version: 'runc version spec: 1.0.1-dev'
  os: linux
  rootless: true
  slirp4netns:
    executable: /usr/bin/slirp4netns
    package: slirp4netns-1.0.1-1.module+el8.2.1+6595+03641d72.x86_64
    version: |-
      slirp4netns version 1.0.1
      commit: 6a7b16babc95b6a3056b33fb45b74a6f62262dd4
      libslirp: 4.3.0
  swapFree: 8493461504
  swapTotal: 8493461504
  uptime: 4h 58m 50.46s (Approximately 0.17 days)
registries:
  search:
  - registry.access.redhat.com
  - registry.redhat.io
  - docker.io
store:
  configFile: /home/burhan/.config/containers/storage.conf
  containerStore:
    number: 0
    paused: 0
    running: 0
    stopped: 0
  graphDriverName: overlay
  graphOptions:
    overlay.mount_program:
      Executable: /usr/bin/fuse-overlayfs
      Package: fuse-overlayfs-1.0.0-2.module+el8.2.1+6465+1a51e8b6.x86_64
      Version: |-
        fuse-overlayfs: version 1.0.0
        FUSE library version 3.2.1
        using FUSE kernel interface version 7.26
  graphRoot: /home/burhan/.local/share/containers/storage
  graphStatus:
    Backing Filesystem: xfs
    Native Overlay Diff: "false"
    Supports d_type: "true"
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
podman-1.9.3-2.module+el8.2.1+6867+366c07d6.x86_64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
I'm assuming the UID of the user you `su`'d to is not 1000?

There are well-known problems with rootless Podman and `sudo`/`su` (they don't count as a full "session" in the eyes of PAM and systemd, so certain things that Podman expects will be created and configured are not), but it looks like we have a different problem here. I suspect that some environment variables (`XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` perhaps?) are being leaked from one user into the other, potentially?

The system has two users; my user (burhan, uid 1000) and the retool user, (uid 1001).

As `retool` is a system user, I login with my user, and then `su` to `retool`. However, to test things out, I enabled ssh and when I ssh directly using `retool`, it works.

I did re-run the shell (executed `/bin/bash` after logging in as retool), because I read somewhere else (forgot where) that this loads the environment correctly while troubleshooting this issue.

However, in the initial scenario - where I ssh as `burhan`, then `su retool`, I also did `/bin/bash` as retool, but that didn't work.

Not sure what's going on to be honest.

if you're using `su` you'll probably want to use `su --login`:

(from `man su`)

```
       -, -l, --login
              Start  the shell as a login shell with an environment similar to
              a real login:

                 o      clears all the environment variables except  TERM  and
                        variables specified by --whitelist-environment

                 o      initializes  the  environment  variables  HOME, SHELL,
                        USER, LOGNAME, and PATH

                 o      changes to the target user's home directory

                 o      sets argv[0] of the shell to '-' in order to make  the
                        shell a login shell
```

otherwise you'll leak XDG environment variables which can affect processes in the other user:

```console
$ env | grep XDG | sort
XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/1000
```

Ah okay. I didn't realize there was an environment variable being used.

Is it going to be an issue if the target user (retool in this case) has no shell? As its just a system user in production they won't have a shell assigned and won't have a home directory either. Its just for permission management.

idk, you'd have to try it -- worst case you can probably utilize `env` (either with `-i` or `-u`)

or use `sudo -u ...` which seems to do a better job than `su`

> Ah okay. I didn't realize there was an environment variable being used.
> 
> Is it going to be an issue if the target user (retool in this case) has no shell? As its just a system user in production they won't have a shell assigned and won't have a home directory either. Its just for permission management.

Not having a home will very likely be an issue as that's where the rootless container storage for each user resides.
@burhan are you ready to go? Can we close?
Yes thank you. This resolves the issue. I need to create a "real" user with a valid home and environment.
